does: This paper introduces the Online Merging Optimizer, a method that integrates parameter merging into the RLHF optimization loop to balance alignment rewards with preservation of pre-training capabilities. The core contribution is performing gradient-based merging at each training step by blending the RL policy gradients with direction vectors derived from parameter differences between SFT and pre-trained models. The authors demonstrate empirically that this approach reduces the alignment tax—performance degradation in general capabilities—while maintaining strong alignment performance across multiple LLM families (Qwen, LLaMA) of varying sizes (1.8B to 8B parameters) and different RLHF algorithms (DPO, KTO).
connects: This work builds on the established observation that post-hoc model merging can interpolate trade-offs between alignment and capability retention in LLMs. It extends prior research on parameter interpolation and the alignment tax problem by moving merging from a post-training operation into the online optimization process itself. The paper connects to broader RLHF methodology including DPO and KTO variants, and responds to the known tension between reward maximization and task performance degradation. It also relates to recent work on efficient alignment and multi-objective optimization in LLM training, where steering optimization trajectories to preserve valuable learned representations has emerged as a key challenge.
enables: This approach opens directions for more sophisticated online optimization strategies that jointly optimize multiple objectives without explicit multi-objective formulations. The method could enable better practical RLHF implementations that reduce computational waste from capability loss during alignment. Future work might extend online merging to other objective functions beyond reward maximization, develop theoretical understanding of why gradient merging preserves SFT directions, or apply similar principles to other fine-tuning scenarios with conflicting objectives. The technique also suggests pathways for combining multiple specialized models or training signals within a single optimization loop, potentially informing continual learning and adaptation in deployed LLMs.
